California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 6, 1988

William Kimberling
Deputy Director
National Clearinghouse
on Election Administration
Federal Election Commission
Washington, D.C. 20463

Re: Your Request for Advice;
Our File No. G-88-079

Dear Mr. Kimberling:

Chairman John Larson asked that I prepare a response to
your request that we review your publication materials and
update them to reflect changes in our statute, regulations or
procedures. We have indicated the changes as you requested and
they are shown on the enclosed copies. Where major changes are
necessary, they are indicated by an insert number. That number
corresponds to the number on the typed "Inserts" document, also
enclosed.

I trust that these materials adequately and accurately
respond to your request. We look forward to receipt of your
new publication, Campaign Finance Law 88. If you have any
questions regarding the enclosures, I suggest that you phone
Ms. Jeanne Pritchard, Chief, Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division. Her telephone number is (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Laidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

Enc:

cc: Chairman Larson
Jeanne Pritchard
Edward D. Fiegenbaum

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • [illegible]

State of California

Memorandum

To : Diane Griffiths                                  Date : February 18, 1988


From : Fair Political Practices Commission
        John Larson, Chairman [illegible]

Subject : Material from Federal Election Commission

        Attached is the material from the Federal Election Commission
asking that we update our position of the biennial report on
State campaign finances laws.  I would appreciate somebody
doing the necessary.

        Thank you.

Attachment

FEDERAL ELECTION COMMISSION
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20463

February 4, 1988

Dear John,

We are requesting your help again in correcting and
updating our biennial report on State campaign finances laws.

This report is one of our best sellers, and I hope it
has reduced somewhat the information demands on your office
-- or at least improved the quality of the questions.
Even though this is a busy season for all of us, we
would appreciate it if you could take a few minutes to review
the enclosed materials and respond to our contractor.

As always, we are grateful for your help in these things
and we will be sending you a copy of Campaign Finance Law
88 as quickly as the contractor receives and integrates your
comments or changes.

Hope all is going well in your preparations for this
year.

Sincerely yours,

William Kimberling
Deputy Director,
National Clearinghouse on
Election Administration

D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc.
Bloomington, Indiana

January 22, 1988

Mr. John H. Larson
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
Suite 800
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Larson:

CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is now being prepared for publication this spring by the
Federal Election Commission's National Clearinghouse on Election Administration.
James A. Palmer and I again are assisting D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc., in
the development of this compendium of state campaign finance laws for the
Clearinghouse. This is our biennial request for your assistance and participation in
the production of this important report.

CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 will present the law on campaign finance regulation in
each jurisdiction as of January 1, 1988, in two convenient formats: (1) a State Law
Summary synopsizing the law and (2) Quick Reference Charts highlighting key
provisions. The 1988 report will cover all major aspects of each jurisdiction's
campaign finance law including:

(1) Contribution and solicitation limitations.
(2) Expenditure limitations.
(3) Reporting requirements.
(4) Tax and public-financing provisions.
(5) Post-election requirements.
(6) Offenses and penalties.
(7) Responsible regulatory or supervisory state agencies.

In order to produce a report with campaign finance law information for California
that is as current, comprehensive, accurate, and useful as possible, we request that
you:

(1) Provide relevant printed materials that are available concerning California's
campaign finance law and its interpretation and administration, such as (a) relevant
statutes, administrative rules or regulations, and state constitutional provisions,
(b) relevant advisory opinions or legal interpretations, (c) important and relevant
state trial and appellate court and federal district court opinions, and (d) and any
other relevant guidance, including forms, instructions, summaries of the law, and
other campaign finance-related information that will provide us with insight into the
campaign finance law and its operation in California. Note: if you provided this
information for CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86, we need only those printed materials that
will bring us up to date as to any changes since January 1, 1986.

(2) Review and critique the State Law Summary and Quick Reference Chart entries
for California contained in CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86 (see enclosure). Please note on
these documents (or in a separate letter if you prefer) any errors, omissions, changes
since January 1, 1986, and suggestions for improvement and return the marked-up
copies.

We realize that we have asked in the past and are again asking a lot of you and
your staff, but it is the only way we can ensure that CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is a
report that is acceptable to you and to us -- a product that is both useful and used
and not one that creates more questions than it answers. Any assistance you may be
able to provide us about California's campaign finance law will be greatly
appreciated. We would be delighted if you could respond to this request within the
next two weeks.

Sincerely,

Edward D. Feigenbaum

Edward D. Feigenbaum

Please direct correspondence to:     Edward D. Feigenbaum
                                    FEC Campaign Finance Law 88 Project
                                    623 White Pine Drive
                                    Noblesville, Indiana 46060

PART I

QUICK REFERENCE CHARTS

Quick-reference charts are presented in this part of the report and pro-
vide a convenient summary of the major provisions of state law concerning cam-
paign finance report filing requirements, contribution, solicitation, and ex-
penditure limitations, and public financing and special tax provisions in ef-
fect through December 31, 1985. More complete and detailed information on
these topics for each jurisdiction is provided in Part II: Summaries of
State Campaign Finance Law.

Chart 1: Campaign Finance Report Filing Requirements. This chart
presents information on campaign finance reporting requirements in each juris-
diction, with an emphasis on the requirements for reporting or disclosing cam-
paign-related contributions and expenditures. The chart identifies who is re-
quired to report ("required from"); the general contents for the reports
("contents"); the time or schedule for filing the reports ("time"); the office
or officer with whom the reports are required to be filed ("filed with"); addi
tional campaign finance-related reports that are required to be filed, such
as a statement of organization or registration statement, the designation of a
campaign treasurer, campaign depository, or principal campaign committee, and
a report of independent expenditures ("other reports or statements required");
and the state or local agencies responsible for the administration and en-
forcement of the campaign finance law in a jurisdiction ("regulatory agen-
cy").

Charts 2-A and 2-B: Contribution and Solicitation Limitations. These
charts summarize the state law provisions concerning limitations on contribu-
tions and the solicitation of contributions. Contribution limitation infor-
mation includes restrictions on contributions to particular recipients
(e.g., candidates and political committees) in particular types of campaigns
or elections (e.g., primary or general election) made by particular types of
contributors: individuals, candidates, family members of candidates, corpora-
tions, labor unions, separate segregated funds (PACs), banks and savings and
loan institutions, regulated industries, governmental officers and employees,
and political parties. Special restrictions on particular methods of making
contributions are presented: cash contributions, anonymous contributions, and
contributions made in the name of another. Limitations on solicitation by
governmental officers and employees are also noted.

Charts 3-A and 3-B: Expenditure Limitations. These charts synopsize
the major provisions concerning election campaign-related expenditures, in-
cluding identification of the individuals who may authorize expenditures, the
permissible amount of expenditures, and any restrictions on when expenditures
may be made, as well as related reporting or filing requirement. The autho-
rized uses for which expenditures may be made, including the personal use of a
candidate, his family, or others, transfer to other political committees, in-
vestment of campaign contributions and funds, and election day expenses, are
arrayed. The law on alternative methods for making expenditures, such as by
cash payment or on credit by credit card, are also presented. A final cat-
egory of "other restrictions" summarizes any special requirements concerning
expenditures that are not listed in any other category.

CHART 1. CAMPAIGN FINANCE REPORT FILING REQUIREMENTS (Continued)

STATE | REQUIRED FROM | CONTENTS | TIME | FILED WITH | OTHER REPORTS OR STATEMENTS REQUIRED | REGULATORY AGENCY
---|---|---|---|---|---|---
Arkansas | Candidates and persons acting on their behalf receiving contributions in excess of $250 per election from any person for candidates for public office other than a school district, township, county, or municipal office. The threshold is $100 for candidates for a school district, township, or municipal office. An unopposed candidate does not have to file a pre-election report for that election. | Contribution reports must include information on all contributions and itemize all contributions in excess of the threshold amount. Expenditure reports must itemize all expenditures by category. | Contributions reports: 25 days (except school district, township, municipal and county offices) 7 days before and 30 days after each election. Expenditures reports: 30 days after each election. Supplemental reports for expenditures after the compilation date of the post-election report. | Secretary of State and with county clerk of county where candidate resides. School district, municipal, township, and county candidates: county clerk of county in which election is held. | | Secretary of State 256 State Capitol Building Little Rock, AR 72201 (501)371-1010
California | Candidates receiving or expending more than $500 in an election; elected officers, committees, if the committee receives contributions of more than $500 in a calendar year, makes more than $5,000 in contributions at the behest of candidates or other committees, or makes $500 or more in independent expenditures in a calendar year. | Information on all contributions and expenditures. Contributions or expenditures of $100 or more must be itemized. | Semi-annual: July 31 and Jan. 31 (candidates and committees, if they made or received contributions or expenditures in previous 6 months); elected officers. Periodic: March 22, Sept. 22, 12 days before 1st Tuesday after 1st Mon. in June and Nov. (required only if $500 threshold amount in reporting period is met). Other: 40 and 12 days before the 65th day after election (if election not held on 1st Tues. after 1st Mon. in June or Nov.). late contributions or indepen- | All officers, candidates, & committees must file: 2 copies with clerk of county in which domiciled. Statewide officers, candidates and committees also file original and 1 copy with Sec. of State, 2 copies each with registrar of Los Angeles and San Francisco. State legislature, board of equalization, court of appeal and superior court judges also file original and 1 copy w/Secy. of State, 2 copies w/clerk of county with largest number of registered voters in district affected. CONTENTS

Information on all contributions and expenditures. Contributions or expenditures during the reporting period in excess of $25 must be itemized. Details of in-kind contributions during the reporting period in excess of $100 by a single contributor must also be disclosed.

Information on all contributions and expenditures. Contributions or expenditures in excess of $25 must be itemized. Details of in-kind contributions during the reporting period in excess of $100 by a single contributor must also be disclosed.

Information on all contributions and expenditures. Contributions in excess of $30 and all expenditures must be itemized.

TIME

dent expenditures of $1,000 or more must be reported within 48 hours. Non-municipal elections: 14 days before and 30 days after regular elections; 11 days before and 30 days after regular elections. Contributions in excess of $500 within 16 days before the election must be reported by the recipient within 48 hrs. after receipt. Supplemental reports are required annually on the anniversary of the elec. until a report shows no unexpended balance or deficit.

Generally: 2nd Thurs. of Jan., Apr., July, Oct.; 7 days before any election; 45 days after general election; 30 days after a primary. Supp. reports: 7 days after distribution of surplus or, if deficit, 90 days after primary or election and then 30 days after increase or decrease in deficit. State central committees: every Jan. 30, Apr. 10, July 10; 12 days before any election.

FILED WITH

other officers, candidates, and committees, as specified.

Non-municipal elections: either secretary of State (statewide, legislative, district, or multi-county candidates) or appropriate county clerk and recorder (other officers). Municipal elections: municipal clerk. Non-statewide multi-county issues: county clerk and recorder of each appropriate county.

Secretary of State; except for municipal political issue committees, with Secretary of State and town clerk; local candidates with town clerk.

OTHER REPORTS OR
STATEMENTS REQUIRED

Candidate's affidavit of family members with Campaign Reform Act committee organization statement.

Designation of campaign treasurer and designation of depository.

[f] - Candidates must meet certain qualifications

PART II

SUMMARIES OF STATE CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAWS

Summaries of the campaign finance law in each state and the District of
Columbia are presented in this part of the report. Each summary is a synopsis
of the state law in effect through December 31, 1985, concerning the following
campaign finance-related subjects:

• Contribution and solicitation limitations

• Expenditure limitations

• Reporting requirements

• Public financing provisions

• Tax provisions

• Post-election requirements

• Offenses and penalties

• Regulatory agencies

The summaries include the most important requirements of the law as found
in statutory codes and, if available, administrative codes, advisory opinions
of the state regulatory agency, court decisions, court decisions, court decisions
and guidance manuals prepared by the state regulatory agency. Since each summary is current through December 31, 1985, and changes in state law after that
date are not included, the reader is advised that it is essential that other
sources be consulted for subsequent changes. It is suggested that the reader
contact the state agency with responsibility for the administration and enforcement of the state's election campaign finance law (names, addresses, and
telephone numbers are included in Chart 1 of the preceding part of the report)
or review the most recent amendments to the state's statutory and administrative codes.

The law concerning state and local elections is covered in each summary;
however, state requirements for Federal elections are also included to the extent that state law has not been superseded by the Federal Election Campaign
Act of 1971, as amended. The provisions of this act and regulations of the
Federal Election Commission supersede and preempt any provision of state law
with respect to federal office, and the Federal Election Commission has regulated that the act supersedes state law concerning the organization and registration of political committees supporting federal office candidates,
disclosure of receipts and expenditures regarding federal office candidates
and political committees, and limitations on contributions and expenditures
regarding federal office candidates and political committees. The act does
not supersede state law providing for a candidate's personal financial disclosure, sure, and copies of reports required to be filed under the act must also be
filed either with the secretary of state of the state or with the state officer who is charged with maintaining state election campaign finance reports.

CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW
IN CALIFORNIA

I. INTRODUCTION

Candidates receiving or expending more than $500 in an election and candidates meeting a certain threshold of contributions and expenditures must file information on all contributions and expenditures at several times during the year, both before and after elections. All contributions and expenditures of $1,000 or more must be reported within 48 hours. All individuals and entities may contribute unlimited amounts to political campaigns. Cash contributions must be less than $100, anonymous contributions must be less than $100 per year from any one donor, and contributions may not be made in the name of another. California offers a $100 per person tax deduction for political contributions and a graduated surcharge that is matched by the state and provided to political parties for party activities and distribution to statewide general election candidates. The Fair Political Practices Commission is responsible for overseeing the campaign finance provisions of the law. A city or county in the State of California is permitted to impose more stringent campaign laws and may limit contributions and expenditures.

II. CONTRIBUTION AND SOLICITATION LIMITATIONS

A. Definition of Contribution. A contribution is a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or enforceable promise to make a payment, except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes [1]. An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee, or elected officer is a contribution unless full and adequate consideration is received for making the expenditure [2]. A contribution includes the purchase of tickets for events such as dinners, luncheons, rallies, and similar fundraising events, the candidate's own money or property used on behalf of his or her candidate, the granting of discounts or rebates not extended to the public generally or the grant of discounts or rebates by television and radio stations and newspapers not extended on an equal basis to all candidates for the same office; and the payment of compensation by any person for the personal services or expenses of any other person if such services vices are rendered or expenses incurred on behalf of a candidate or committee without payment of full and adequate consideration [1]. A contribution also includes a transfer of anything of value to a committee from another committee unless full and adequate consideration is received [2].

A contribution does not include an amount received pursuant to an enforceable promise to the extent such amounts have been previously reported as a contribution [5]. A contribution does not include a payment made by an occupant of a home or office for costs related to any meeting or fundraising event held in the occupant's home or office if the costs for the meeting or fundraising event is $500 or less [6]. A contribution also does not include volunteer personal services or a payment made by any individual for his or her travel expenses if such payments are made voluntarily without any understanding or agreement that they will be, directly, or indirectly, repaid [7].

A merchant may permit a candidate to give the district coupons which provide certain discounts when presented in connection with the purchase of certain goods and services has not made a contribution because the merchant receives full and adequate consideration in the form of free advertising, the prospect of volume sales, and the possibility a customer will purchase other goods and services when redeeming the coupons [8].

B. Limitations on Nature of Contributions. No contribution of $100 or more may be made or received in cash [9]. A cash contribution is not deemed received if it is not negotiated or deposited and is returned to the contributor within 72 hours of receipt of the campaign statement on which the contribution would otherwise be reported [10]. If a cash contribution is negotiated or deposited, it must be demanded return received within 72 hours of receipt [11]. In the case of any contribution, including a loan which totals in the aggregate $1,000 or more that is made by or received by a candidate, a controlled committee, or a committee formed or existing primarily to support a candidate or measure before the date of the measure which the candidate or measure is to be voted on but after the closing date of the last campaign statement to be filed before the election [12], it is not deemed received if it is returned to the contributor within 48 hours of receipt [13]. No contribution of $100 or more other than a contribution may be made except by written instrument containing the name of the donor and the name of the recipient [14]. The value of an in-kind contribution of $100 or more must be reported in writing to the recipient upon the written request of the recipient [15].

A contribution may be made directly, but by any person other than by the name by which such person is identified for legal purposes [16]. No person may make a contribution on behalf of another, or while acting as the intermediary or agent of another, without disclosure to the recipient of the contribution both his full name and street address, occupation, and the name of his employer. If any, or the principal place of business. If he is self-employed, and the full name and street address, occupation and the name of employer, if any of the principal place of business if self-employed, of the other person [17]. No person may make an anonymous contribution or contributions to a candidate, committee, or any other person totaling $100 or more in a calendar year [18]. An anonymous contribution of $100 or more must not be held by the intended recipient, but instead must be promptly paid to the Secretary of State for deposit in the general fund of the state [19].

All contributions received by a person acting as an agent of a candidate or committee must be reported, not later than the closing date of any campaign statement the committee or candidate for whom the contribution is intended is required to file, to the candidate, committee's treasurer, or any of his designated agents [20]. No contribution may be commingled with the personal funds of the recipient or any other person or placed in any account other than a separate account in the state capital, in any state office building, or in any office for which the state pays the majority of the rent other than a legislative district office [22].

No contribution may be accepted by a committee when there is a vacancy in the office of treasurer [23].

CA-1                                                CA-2

C. Limitations on Solicitations. No officer of an agency may accept,
solicit, or direct a contribution of $250 dollars or more, from any entity, or
his agent, or from any particular or his agent, while a proceeding involving
a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the agency
and for three months following the date a final decision is rendered in the
proceeding, provided, however, that the officer knows or has reason to know
that the participant has a financial interest [24]. This prohibition applies
regardless of whether the officer accepts, solicits, directs the
contribution for himself or on behalf of any candidate for office [25]. If an
officer receives a contribution to be used in a federal, state, or local
election which would otherwise require disqualification, returns the
contribution within thirty days from the time he knows or should have known
about the contribution and the proceeding involves a license, permit, or other
entitlement for use, he is permitted participate in the proceeding [26].

While, in general, no restriction may be placed on the political activities of any officer or employee of a state or local agency [27], no one who
holds or is seeking election or appointment to, any office or employment in a
state or local agency shall, directly or indirectly, use, promise, threaten,
or attempt to use, any office, authority, or influence, whether those possessed
or merely anticipated, to confer upon or secure for any individual person, or
to aid or obstruct any individual person from securing and position, nomination, confirmation, promotion, or change in compensation of position, within
the state or local agency, upon consideration or the condition that the vote or
political influence or action of such person or another must be given or used
in behalf of, or withheld from, any candidate, officer, or party, or upon any
other corrupt condition or consideration [28]. This prohibition must apply to
urging or discouraging the individual employee's active [29].

An officer or employee of a state or local agency is not prohibited from
soliciting or receiving political funds or contributions to promote the passage or defeat of a ballot measure which would affect the rate of pay, hours
of work, retirement, civil service, or other working conditions of officers or
employees of such state or local agency, except that a state or local agency
may prohibit or limit such activities by its employees during their working
hours, and may prohibit or limit entry into governmental offices for such purposes during hours [30].

III. EXPENDITURE LIMITATIONS

A. Definition of Expenditure. An expenditure is a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
that it is not made for political purposes [31]. An independent expenditure
is an expenditure made by any person in connection with a communication which
expressly advocates the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate,
or the qualification, passage, or defeat of a clearly identified measure, or
taken as a whole and in context, unambiguously purports as a particular result in
election but which is not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate
or committee [32].

B. Limitations on Nature of Expenditures. No expenditure of $100 or
more may be made in cash [33]. No expenditure may be made, other than

overhead or normal operating expenses, by an agent or independent contractor,
including, but not limited to, an advertising agency, on behalf of, or for the
benefit of any candidate or committee unless it is reported by the candidate
or committee as if the expenditure were made directly by the candidate or
committee [34]. The agent or independent contractor must be known to the
candidate or committee all information required to be reported [35].

When an expenditure is made to support litigation aimed at gaining a
place on the ballot for a candidate or measure, aimed at keeping a candidate or measure off the ballot, or challenging the results or an election, the
expenditure is made for the purpose of influencing the outcome of the election
in favor of or against a particular candidate or measure, and should be
reported on the appropriate statement as an expenditure [36].

No expenditure may be made by or on behalf of a committee without the
authorization of the treasurer or that of its designated agents [37]. No
expenditure may be made by or on behalf of a committee there is a vacancy
in the office of treasurer [38].

The payment of a salary, reimbursement for personal expenses, or other
compensation by an employer to an employee who spends more than ten percent of
his compensated time in any one month rendering services for political purposes is an expenditure by the employer if the services are rendered at the
request or direction of the employer, or the employee, with consent of the
employer is relieved of any normal working responsibilities related to his
employment in order to render the personal services, unless the employer
engages in political activity on bona fide, although comparable, vacation
time, or pursuant to a uniform policy allowing employees to engage in political activity [39]. Personal services are rendered for political purposes if
they are carried on for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of one or
more candidates, or the qualification or passage of any measure, and include,
but are not limited to, personal services received by or made at the behest of
a candidate or committee by an employee, and that event developing or distributing communications that expressly advocate the election or defeat of a
clearly identified candidate, or the qualification, passage, or defeat of a
clearly identified measure [40]. The amount of such an expenditure is the pro
rata portion of the gross salary, reimbursement for personal expenses, or
compensation attributable to the time spent on political activity [41].
Salary, reimbursement for personal expenses, and compensation paid to an
employee by an employer who has contracted to provide services to a candidate
or committee are not an expenditure by the employer, provided that the services rendered by the employee are not beyond the scope of the contract [42].

IV. REPORTING REQUIREMENTS

A. General Requirements. Any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly receives contributions totaling $500 or more in a
calendar year [43]; makes independent expenditures totaling $500 or more in a
calendar year [44]; or makes contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees [45]; becomes a
committee required to file campaign statements under the act.

CA-3                                                                                                               CA-4

B. Registration. A committee that receives contribution of $500 or more in a calendar year is a recipient committee and must file a statement of organization with the secretary of state within 10 days after it has qualified as a committee [64]. The statement of organization must include the committee's name, street address, and telephone number, if any [47]; the name, street address, and telephone number, if any of each person, if any, with which the committee is affiliated or connected [48], and the committee's treasurer and principal officers [49]; and the full name and office sought by any candidate and the title and ballot number, if any, of any measure which the committee reports or opposes as its primary activity [50]. A committee that does not support or oppose one or more candidates or ballot measure is primarily activity must provide a brief description of its political activities, including whether it supports or opposes candidates or measures and whether such candidates or measures have common characteristics such as a first political party affiliation [51]. The statement of organization must also include a statement whether the committee is independent or controlled, and if it is controlled, the name of each candidate or committee by which it is controlled or with which it acts jointly [52]; and information on the disposition of surplus funds which will be made in the event of dissolution [53]. Whenever there is a change in any of the information contained in a statement of organization, an amendment must be filed within 10 days to reflect the change [54].

C. Reporting by Candidates and Committees. All candidates and committees must file campaign statements no later than July 31st and January 31st if they have made or received contributions or made any expenditures during the six calendar months before the closing date of such statements [55], June 30th [56], and December 30th [57], unless a candidate files a declaration on or before the filing date for the first campaign statement due before the election, signed by the candidate under penalty of perjury, stating that to the best of the candidate's knowledge less than $500 in contributions or expenditures will be received or made in behalf of or in support of his candidacy [58]. If $500 in contributions or expenditures are eventually received or made, the candidate must file campaign statements as otherwise required [59]. All elected officials must file campaign no later than July 31st and January 31st, except for judges or elected officers whose salaries are less than $100 a month, unless the elected officer or judge is a candidate or committee which makes or receives contributions or makes expenditures during the applicable six-month period [60]. In lieu of the filing of a campaign statement by a candidate or elected officer, the candidate or elected officer may file a statement signed under penalty of perjury that, to the best of his knowledge, less than $500 has been or will be received or expended on behalf of or in support of his candidacy [61]. The statement shall also contain the name, street address, and treasurer of any committee of which he has knowledge which has received contributions or made expenditures on behalf of his candidacy, whether the committee is controlled by the candidate or officeholder [62]. In calculating whether $500 in expenditures have been made, payments for a filing fee or for the costs of a statement of organization made from the candidate's personal funds must not be included [63].

In addition to the required six-month campaign statements, candidates, officeholders, and all committees must file up to four "threshold" campaign statements if they raise or spend $500 since the last report, except that the candidate and supporting committees need not file threshold statements during any six-month period in which the candidate or officeholder is not being voted upon [64]. A candidate, elected officer, controlled committee, and any committee formed primarily to support or oppose a candidate or a measure that has made or received contributions or made expenditures of $500 or more during the specified "threshold" periods, and all other committees which, in an even-numbered year, has made or received contributions of $500 or more, or made expenditures of $500 or more during the threshold periods, or in an odd-numbered year, has made contributions or independent expenditures of $500 or more during the threshold periods must file a campaign statement not later than March 22nd, with a $500 threshold period of January 1st through March 17th [65]; no later than 12 days before the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June, with a $500 threshold period of March 18th through 17 days before the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June [66], September 22nd, with a $500 threshold period of July 1st through September 17th [67]; and 12 days before the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November, with a $500 threshold period of September 18th through 17 days before the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November [68]. Elected officers, candidates, and their controlled committees and committees formed primarily to support or oppose a candidate or ballot measure are not required to file threshold campaign statements during any January 1-June 30 or July 1-December 31 period in which the candidate's office or the ballot measure is not to be voted on or, in the case of an elected officer, when the elected officer is not a candidate for an office being voted on in a six-month period, unless they made contributions or expenditures of $100 or more to any other candidate, controlled committee formed primarily to support or oppose a candidate or ballot measure [69].

Candidates, their controlled committees, and committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose ballot measures in an election held other than on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June or November, must file a campaign statement not later than 40 days before the election, no later than 12 days before the election, and no later than 65 days after the runoff election [70]. The closing date for each of the pre-election campaign statements is five days prior to the filing deadline [71]. If a subsequent runoff election is held within 60 days of the first election, the post-election statement must be filed 65 days after the runoff election [72]. If, however, a candidate or committee is required to file a campaign statement in connection with an election held on a date other than the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June or September 22nd, if the first election is on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November, no later than 12 days prior to the first election, no later than 12 days prior to the runoff election, and no later than 65 days after the runoff election [74]. Filers under this section do not file a threshold statement as is held [75]; any candidate or committee that has filed a pre-election campaign statement in connection with any election held on a date other than the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June or September 22nd, if the first election is on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November, may designate that such campaign statement as final and need not file any further campaign statements in connection with that election if the candidate or committee has made no expenditures and received no contributions since

CA-5                                                                                                    CA-6

the closing date of the campaign statement designated as final [76]; the candidate or committee has no intentions of making any expenditures or receiv­ing any contributions in the period between the closing date of the campaign statement designated as final and the closing date of the post-election state­ment otherwise required to be filed [77]; and, in the case of a candidate, no committee is making expenditures or receiving contributions on behalf of or in support of his candidate [78]. Prior to the date for filing the next campaign statement the candidate or committee would otherwise be required to file, the candidate or committee must execute a declaration stating compliance with these provisions, and file it in the same manner and place as a campaign statement [79]. A candidate or committee is required to file the required campaign statements if the committee or candidate makes expenditures or receives contributions after the closing date of the campaign statement desig­nated as final [80].

A committee formed or existing primarily to support or oppose the quali­fication of a measure, and proponents of a state measure must file campaign statements not later than 35 days after the deadline for filing petitions or the date of notification that the measure has either qualified or failed to qualify, whichever is earlier [81], with a closing date of seven days before the deadline [82]. Such a committee must not be required to file a semi­annual campaign statement until after the filing date specified for ballot measure committees and proponents [83].

Each candidate or committee that makes or receives a late contribution aggregating $1,000 or more from a single source must report the late contribution to each office with which the candidate or committee is required to file its next campaign statement [84] by mailgram, telegram, personal delivery or other overnight guaranteed delivery service or personal delivery within 48 hours of the time it is made in the case of the candidate or committee that makes the contribution, and within 48 hours of the time it is received in the case of the recipient [85]. A late contribution must be reported on subse­quent campaign statements without regard to this filing [86]. A late contri­bution need not be reported, nor will it be deemed accepted, if it is not cashed, negotiated, or deposited, and is returned to the contributor within 48 hours of its receipt [87].

A committee that makes independent expenditures totaling $500 or more in a calendar year to support or oppose a candidate, a measure, or qualification of a measure must file independent expenditure reports at the same times as a committee formed or existing primarily to support or oppose such candidate, measure, or qualification of such measure [88], with the same closing date [89]. The period covered by an independent expenditure report related to a candidate or measure begins on the day after the closing date of the most recent independent expenditure report related to that candidate or measure, and end on the closing date of the report in question [90]. If no previous independent expenditure report has been filed in connection with the election, the period covered begins on January 1st of the year in which the report is filed [91].

A committee that makes a late independent expenditure aggregating $1,000 or more from a single source must report the late contribution to each office with which the candidate or committee is required to file its next campaign statement [84] by mailgram, telegram, or personal delivery within 48 hours of the time it is made [92]. The places where it would be reported to file campaign statements if it were formed or existing primarily to support or oppose the candidate or measure or against such it is making the late expenditure [93]. A late independent expenditure must be reported on subsequent campaign statements [94].

A special election campaign report is required of a committee that makes contributions of $15,000 or more to support or oppose candidates or state mea­sures voted upon in a special election held on a date other than the first Tuesday after the first Monday in June or November [95]. The reports are to be filed at the same time as required of a committee formed or existing primarily to support or oppose such candidates or measures, if not otherwise required to file campaign statements in connection with that special election [96], with the same closing dates [97]. The report period covered for the first required report must begin on the day after the closing day of the previous report filed for that special election [98].

A candidate or state measure proponent and any committee or committees which the candidate or a state measure proponent controls may file consolidated campaign statements in each place each of the committees and the candidate or state measure proponent would be required to file campaign statements if separate statements are filed [99].

An organization which sponsors a political action committee is not a political committee and is not required to file a campaign statement if the name of the political action committee includes the official or public-ly-rec­ognized name of the sponsoring organization [100]; the sponsoring organization does not make a sufficient amount of contributions and expenditures, other than those in support of the committee, to satisfy the reporting thresholds [101]; the committee reports all contributions and expenditures in sup­port of the committee by the sponsoring organization, its intermediate units, and the numbers of such entities [102]; the committee reports as an intermedi­ate sponsoring organization and each intermediate unit which directly or indirectly provides the committee with $100 or more in number contributions, regardless of whether any number for whom the intermediary acted contributed $100 or more [103]. If the intermediate unit's name is substantially different from the name of the sponsoring organization [104] and a responsible officer of the sponsoring organization verifies the campaign statement in addition to the treasurer of the political action committee [105].

If a state or local government agency has enough campaign activity to qualify it as a committee, the state or local government agency itself will be required to file the campaign statements [106]. The individual authorized in directing the making of expenditures or contributions which qualify an agency as a committee must be the treasurer unless another individual is designated [107]. Under many circumstances, campaign-related activities by a state or local government agency may be illegal [108].

D. Reporting by Others. Any person who employs or contracts for the services of one or more lobbyists, independently or jointly with another

CA-7                                                                                                                              CA-8

[109] and any person who directly or indirectly makes payments to influence
legislative or administrative action of 12,500 or more in value in any
calendar quarter [110] must file periodic reports detailing the date and
amount of each contribution of 25 or more made by the filer to a state
candidate, an elected state officer, or a committee supporting such candidate
or officer, and the name of the recipient of each contribution [111].

E. Contents of Campaign Statements. Each campaign statement must
contain information on the total amount of contributions received and
expenditures made during the period covered by the campaign statement, and the
total cumulative amount of contributions received and expenditures made [112];
the total amount of contributions received during the period from persons who
have given a cumulative amount of $100 or more [113], and the total amount of
contributions received during the period from persons who have given a
cumulative amount of less than $100 [114]; and the balance of cash and cash
equivalents on hand at the beginning and end of the period covered by the
campaign statement [115]. If the cumulative amount of contributions
(including loans) received from a person is $100 or more and a contribution or
loan has been received from that person during the period covered by the
campaign statement, the statement must detail that person's full name [116];
the name of the person's employer, or, if self-employed, name of business
[117]; the date and amount received for each contribution received during the
period, and, if the contribution is a loan, the interest rate [118]; and the
cumulative amount of the contributions [119]. The full name [120]; street
address [121]; occupation [122]; the name of the person's employer, or, if
self-employed, name of business [123]; and the amount of a person's maximum
liability required to be on the statement for each person, other than the
filer, who is directly, indirectly, or contingently liable for repayment of a
loan received during the period covered by the campaign statement must also be
included [124].

The statement must also contain the total amount of expenditures made
during the period to persons who have received $100 or more [125], and to persons who have received less than $100 [126]. For each person to whom an
expenditure of $100 or more was made during the period covered by [127], and each
person, if different from the payee, who has provided consideration for an
expenditure of $100 or more during the period [128], the statement must contain the person's full name [129]; street address [130]; the amount of each
expenditure [131]; a brief description of the consideration for which the
expenditure was made [132]; and, in the case of an expenditure that is a contribution, the date of the contribution, cumulative contributions made to a candidate, elected officer, or committee; the full name of the
candidate and the office and district for which he seeks nomination or election, and, in the case of a contribution in support of or opposition to a
measure, the number or letter of the measure and the jurisdiction in which the
measure voted on [133]. If the campaign statement is filed in connection with
the qualification of a measure, it must contain the name, street address,
amount of expenditure, and a brief description of the consideration for which
each expenditure was made for each person who directly, indirectly, or
through an intermediary received payments cumulatively totaling $100 or more
for circulation of petitions to qualify a measure for the ballot [134].

In the case of a controlled committee, an official committee of a political party, or an organization formed or existing primarily for political
purposes, the amount and source of any miscellaneous receipts must be disclosed
[135]. If a contribution is made to or received from a committee, or an
expenditure made to a committee, the statement must list the number assigned
by the Secretary of State to the committee, or, if no number has been
assigned, the full name and street address of the treasurer of the committee
[136]. In a campaign statement filed by a committee supporting or opposing
more than one candidate or measure, the amount of expenditures of $100 or more
for or against each candidate or measure during the period, and the cumulative
amount of expenditures of $100 or more for or against each such candidate or
measure must be reported [137]. In a campaign statement filed by a candidate
or who is a candidate in both an election held on the first Tuesday after the
first Monday in June, and an election held on the first Tuesday after the
first Monday in November, the candidate's controlled committees, or a committee
primarily formed to support such a candidate, the total amount of contributions received and expenditures made for the period January 1st through June
30th, and the total amount of contributions received and expenditures made for
the period July 1st through December 31st are to be reported [138].

The campaign statement must also contain the full name, residential and
business addresses and telephone numbers of the filer, or, in the case of a
campaign statement filed by a committee, the name, street address, and telephone number of the committee and its treasurer [139]. If a campaign statement is filed by a candidate who has received the maximum amount, street
address, and treasurer of any committee of which he has knowledge which has
received contributions or made expenditures on behalf of his or her candidacy,
and whether such committee is controlled by the candidate [140]. A candidate
must verify his campaign statement, and the campaign statement of each committee subject to his own control. All reports, even those of noncandidates, are
to be signed under penalty of perjury [141]. The verification shall state
that the filer has used all reasonable diligence in its preparation, and, that
to the best of his knowledge it is true and complete [142]. In the case of a
candidate or state measure proponent verification, it must state that to the
best of the individual's knowledge, and that the treasurer of each controlled
committee used all reasonable diligence in the preparation of the committee's
statement, but the treasurer must also verify the report [143]. A report or
statement filed by a committee shall be signed and verified by the treasurer,
and a report or statement filed by any other organization shall be signed and
verified by a responsible officer of the organization, or by an attorney or a
certified public accountant [144].

An independent expenditure report must contain the name, street address,
and telephone number of the committee and of the committee's treasurer, and
the number assigned to the committee by the secretary of state [145]. If the
report is related to a candidate, the full name of the candidate and the
office and district for which the candidate seeks nomination or election, and
if the report is related to a measure or qualification of a measure, the number or letter of the measure, or if none has yet been assigned, a brief description of the subject matter of the measure, and the jurisdiction in which
the measure is to be voted on or would be voted on if it qualified [146]; and
the total amount of expenditures related to the candidate or measure during
the period made by persons who have receives less than $100 [147], and more

CA-9                                                                                CA-10

Dear Mr. Larson: 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is now being prepared for publication this spring by the 
Federal Election Commission's National Clearinghouse on Election Administration. 
james A. Palmer and I again are assisting D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc., in 
the development of this compendium of state campaign finance laws for the 
Clearinghouse. 
This is our biennial request for your assistance and participation in 
the production of this important report. 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 will present the law on campaign finance regulation in 
each jurisdiction as of january 1, 1988, in two convenient formats: 
(1) a State Law
sections.conclusion:
synopsizing the law and (2) Quick Reference Charts highlighting key 
provisions. 
The 1988 report will cover all major aspects of each jurisdiction's 
campaign finance law including: 
(1) 
Contribution and solicitation limitations. 
(2) 
Expenditure limitations. 
(3) 
Reporting requirements. 
(4) 
Tax and public-financing provisions. 
(5) 
Post-election requirements. 
(6) 
Offenses and penalties. 
(7) 
Responsible regulatory or supervisory state agencies. 
In order to produce a report with campaign finance law information for California 
that is as current, comprehensive, accurate, and useful as possible, we request that 
you; 
(1) Provide relevant printed materials that are available concerning California's 
campaign finance law and its interpretation and administration, such as (a) relevant 
statutes, administrative 
rules 
or

state 
constitutional

relevant 
state trial and appellate court and federal district court opinil?ns, and (d) and any

summarIes of the 
law, and 
other campaign finance-related information, that will provide us with insight into the

Dear Mr. Larson: 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is now being prepared for publication this spring by the 
Federal Election Commission's National Clearinghouse on Election Administration. 
james A. Palmer and I again are assisting D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc., in 
the development of this compendium of state campaign finance laws for the 
Clearinghouse. 
This is our biennial request for your assistance and participation in 
the production of this important report. 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 will present the law on campaign finance regulation in 
each jurisdiction as of january 1, 1988, in two convenient formats: 
(1) a State Law

CONCLUSION: synopsizing the law and (2) Quick Reference Charts highlighting key 
provisions. 
The 1988 report will cover all major aspects of each jurisdiction's 
campaign finance law including: 
(1) 
Contribution and solicitation limitations. 
(2) 
Expenditure limitations. 
(3) 
Reporting requirements. 
(4) 
Tax and public-financing provisions. 
(5) 
Post-election requirements. 
(6) 
Offenses and penalties. 
(7) 
Responsible regulatory or supervisory state agencies. 
In order to produce a report with campaign finance law information for California 
that is as current, comprehensive, accurate, and useful as possible, we request that 
you; 
(1) Provide relevant printed materials that are available concerning California's 
campaign finance law and its interpretation and administration, such as (a) relevant 
statutes, administrative 
rules 
or 
regulations, and 
state 
constitutional 
provisions, 
(b) relevant advisory OpInIOnS or legal interpretations, (c) important and 
relevant 
state trial and appellate court and federal district court opinil?ns, and (d) and any 
other relevant guidance, including forms, instructions, 
summarIes of the 
law, and 
other campaign finance-related information, that will provide us with insight into the 
campaign finance law and its operation in California.

If you provided this 
information for CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86, we need only those printed materials that

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission April 6, 1988 William Kimberling Deputy Director National Clearinghouse on Election Administration Federal Election Commission Washington, D.C. 20463 Re: Your Request for Advice; Our File No. G-88-079 Dear Mr. Kimberling: Chairman John Larson asked that I prepare a response to your request that we review your publication materials and update them to reflect changes in our statute, regulations or procedures. We have indicated the changes as you requested and they are shown on the enclosed copies. Where major changes are necessary, they are indicated by an insert number. That number corresponds to the number on the typed "Inserts" document, also enclosed. I trust that these materials adequately and accurately respond to your request. We look forward to receipt of your new publication, Campaign Finance Law 88. If you have any questions regarding the enclosures, I suggest that you phone Ms. Jeanne Pritchard, Chief, Technical Assistance and Analysis Division. Her telephone number is (916) 322-5662. Sincerely, Robert E. Laidigh Counsel, Legal Division Enc: cc: Chairman Larson Jeanne Pritchard Edward D. Fiegenbaum 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • [illegible] State of California Memorandum To : Diane Griffiths Date : February 18, 1988 From : Fair Political Practices Commission John Larson, Chairman [illegible] Subject : Material from Federal Election Commission Attached is the material from the Federal Election Commission asking that we update our position of the biennial report on State campaign finances laws. I would appreciate somebody doing the necessary. Thank you. This report is one of our best sellers, and I hope it has reduced somewhat the information demands on your office -- or at least improved the quality of the questions. Even though this is a busy season for all of us, we would appreciate it if you could take a few minutes to review the enclosed materials and respond to our contractor. As always, we are grateful for your help in these things and we will be sending you a copy of Campaign Finance Law 88 as quickly as the contractor receives and integrates your comments or changes. Hope all is going well in your preparations for this year. Sincerely yours, William Kimberling Deputy Director, National Clearinghouse on Election Administration D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc.
